heroOverview: Hyperoxaluria (ICD-10: E72.53) is a metabolic condition characterised by abnormally high urinary oxalate excretion — conventionally defined as more than 40 mg (0.44 mmol) per day in adults — that predisposes the kidneys to calcium oxalate crystal deposition, stone formation, and, in severe cases, progressive renal failure. The condition spans a wide severity spectrum: enteric hyperoxaluria from fat malabsorption is common and affects 15-40% of patients after gastric bypass surgery, while the primary genetic forms are rare, with primary hyperoxaluria type 1 estimated at 1-3 per million population globally. Calcium oxalate nephrolithiasis is the most frequent clinical consequence, but high-grade oxalate excretion can deposit crystals throughout the kidney (nephrocalcinosis) and, if renal clearance falls, in bone, heart, retina, and vessels — a systemic complication called oxalosis.

keyStats.prevalence: Primary hyperoxaluria type 1: approximately 1-3 per million (European data); enteric hyperoxaluria affects up to 40% of patients after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass
keyStats.demographics: Primary forms present in childhood; enteric forms in adults after bariatric surgery or inflammatory bowel disease; dietary form in all ages
keyStats.avgAge: Primary hyperoxaluria type 1: first stone event commonly in the first two decades; enteric hyperoxaluria: adult onset after surgery or gut disease
keyStats.globalCases: Calcium oxalate stones (a frequent consequence) account for roughly 80% of all kidney stones worldwide

definition: Hyperoxaluria (ICD-10: E72.53) is defined by urinary oxalate excretion persistently above 40 mg per day and is classified by its cause. Primary hyperoxaluria encompasses three autosomal recessive inborn errors of hepatic oxalate metabolism: type 1 (AGXT gene deficiency, the most common and most severe), type 2 (GRHPR gene deficiency), and type 3 (HOGA1 gene deficiency). In primary hyperoxaluria type 1, deficiency of liver-specific alanine-glyoxylate aminotransferase (AGT) causes accumulation of glyoxylate, which is then oxidised to oxalate; urinary excretion may exceed 250 mg per day. Enteric hyperoxaluria is far more common clinically and arises when fat malabsorption (from inflammatory bowel disease, coeliac disease, short bowel syndrome, or bariatric surgery) leaves excess fatty acids in the colon that bind calcium, freeing oxalate for colonic absorption; urinary oxalate typically ranges from 50 to 120 mg per day. Dietary hyperoxaluria results from excessive consumption of oxalate-rich foods and is usually mild. Once urinary oxalate exceeds the solubility product with urinary calcium, calcium oxalate crystals form in the collecting system, causing stones and, with prolonged high-grade excretion, nephrocalcinosis. As glomerular filtration declines, plasma oxalate rises and crystals deposit in extra-renal tissues, producing systemic oxalosis.

typesClassification.description: The most common and most severe genetic form, caused by AGXT gene mutations. Hepatic AGT deficiency leads to massive oxalate overproduction; kidney failure in childhood or early adulthood is common without treatment.
Caused by GRHPR gene deficiency; urinary oxalate excretion is lower than PH1 and progression to kidney failure slower, but recurrent stones and nephrocalcinosis occur.
The mildest genetic form, caused by HOGA1 gene mutations. Urinary oxalate is modestly elevated; significant kidney damage is uncommon but stones occur.

The most clinically prevalent form, caused by fat malabsorption from Crohn disease, coeliac disease, short bowel syndrome, or bariatric surgery; colon oxalate hyperabsorption drives elevated excretion.
Mild transient elevation from excessive spinach, rhubarb, nuts, or tea consumption in individuals without malabsorption or genetic defects; usually resolves with dietary modification.

primarySymptoms: Renal colic — sudden, severe flank or loin pain radiating to the groin caused by a calcium oxalate stone passing from the kidney into the ureter.
Haematuria — blood in the urine, which may be visible (pink or red urine) or detected only on a urine dipstick, associated with stone movement or crystal-induced mucosal injury.
Recurrent kidney stones, often beginning in childhood in primary forms, and frequently bilateral, distinguishing hyperoxaluria from the commoner single-stone episode of dietary origin.
Urinary symptoms during stone passage — frequency, urgency, and burning during urination caused by stone irritation of the bladder and urethra.
Nephrocalcinosis-related gradual decline in kidney function, which may first manifest as fatigue, reduced urine output, ankle swelling, or raised creatinine found incidentally.
Bone pain and pathological fractures in advanced systemic oxalosis, when calcium oxalate crystals deposit in trabecular bone, impair bone remodelling, and cause a painful oxalate arthropathy.
Cardiac arrhythmia and conduction defects in end-stage systemic oxalosis, when oxalate crystals deposit in the myocardium and conduction system.
Visual disturbance from retinal oxalate crystal deposition, occasionally the presenting feature of systemic oxalosis in a child with unrecognised primary hyperoxaluria.
Peripheral vascular insufficiency — Raynaud-like livedo reticularis and peripheral ischaemia from oxalate crystal deposition in vessel walls in systemic oxalosis.
Symptoms of chronic kidney disease in advanced primary hyperoxaluria: uraemic symptoms including nausea, vomiting, itching, poor appetite, and anaemia.
earlyWarningSigns: First kidney stone before age 20, especially if bilateral or rapidly recurrent.
Microscopic haematuria detected on a routine urine test in a young person without other explanation.
A second stone within 12 months of the first in an adult — recurrence at this rate warrants metabolic stone work-up.
Persistently elevated urine calcium-to-creatinine ratio on a spot urine in a child presenting with abdominal pain.
emergencySigns: Obstructive uropathy from a stone in a solitary kidney or bilateral obstructing stones — a urological emergency causing acute kidney injury.
Urosepsis in the setting of an obstructing stone with fever and systemic inflammatory response — requires emergency urological decompression.
Sudden decrease in urine output in a known primary hyperoxaluria patient, suggesting acute oxalate nephropathy requiring immediate nephrology review.
Cardiac arrhythmia or syncope in a patient with known systemic oxalosis — potentially life-threatening crystal-induced conduction disturbance.

causes.description: Mutations in the AGXT gene eliminate hepatic alanine-glyoxylate aminotransferase (AGT) activity, blocking the conversion of glyoxylate to glycine. Glyoxylate is instead oxidised to oxalate, which is excreted in urine at rates sometimes exceeding 250 mg per day — up to ten times normal.

causes.cause: GRHPR and HOGA1 gene defects (PH2 and PH3)
causes.description: Deficiencies of glyoxylate reductase/hydroxypyruvate reductase (GRHPR) and 4-hydroxy-2-oxoglutarate aldolase (HOGA1) represent less common and generally milder inborn errors of glyoxylate metabolism that still produce significant oxalate overproduction and stone disease.

In enteric hyperoxaluria, unabsorbed long-chain fatty acids bind calcium in the colon, reducing the calcium available to precipitate oxalate and leaving free oxalate for absorption. The colon epithelium is far more permeable to oxalate than the small intestine, resulting in urinary oxalate excretion that can double or triple.

causes.cause: Inflammatory bowel disease and short bowel
causes.description: Active Crohn disease, extensive small bowel resection, and short bowel syndrome impair ileal fat absorption by mechanisms ranging from bile salt depletion to surface area loss, and they increase colonic oxalate exposure; hyperoxaluria occurs in roughly 20-30% of these patients.

Roux-en-Y gastric bypass bypasses significant small intestinal absorptive area, creating chronic fat malabsorption sufficient to trigger enteric hyperoxaluria in 15-40% of patients; post-bariatric calcium oxalate stone risk is approximately three to four times that of the general population.

Spinach, rhubarb, almonds, peanuts, chocolate, and strong black tea provide large oxalate loads; in individuals with low calcium intake or unusual gut absorption, dietary oxalate alone can push urinary oxalate above the threshold for mild disease.

Pyridoxal phosphate is an essential cofactor for AGT. Functional vitamin B6 deficiency or severely low dietary intake amplifies glyoxylate to oxalate conversion even in individuals with partial AGT function, and is the physiological basis for pyridoxine-responsive primary hyperoxaluria type 1.

riskFactors.factor: Biallelic AGXT, GRHPR, or HOGA1 mutations
riskFactors.description: Autosomal recessive inheritance; two defective copies of the causative gene are required for primary hyperoxaluria. Parents who are carriers are usually asymptomatic but have a 25% chance of an affected child in each pregnancy.

The most common modifiable risk factor in adults; fat malabsorption from bypass surgery raises urinary oxalate in up to 40% of patients. Pre-operative calcium intake optimisation and post-operative dietary oxalate restriction mitigate risk.

Active ileal Crohn disease impairs fat and bile salt absorption; about 20-30% of patients with extensive ileal disease develop hyperoxaluria. Disease control with appropriate IBD therapy reduces the risk.

Dietary calcium binds oxalate in the gut, preventing its absorption. A low-calcium diet paradoxically raises urinary oxalate by releasing more free oxalate into the colon and small intestine for absorption.

Frequent consumption of spinach, rhubarb, nuts, and strong tea raises the oxalate load presented to the gut; in susceptible individuals this drives dietary hyperoxaluria or worsens primary and enteric forms.

Low urine output concentrates all lithogenic factors; adequate hydration dilutes urinary oxalate and raises the threshold for crystal supersaturation. Urine output of at least 2-2.5 litres per day is the primary preventive recommendation across all hyperoxaluria types.
Untreated coeliac disease causes fat malabsorption through villous atrophy; a strict gluten-free diet typically normalises oxalate excretion by restoring small bowel absorptive function.

affectedDemographics: Children and young adults with primary hyperoxaluria, often presenting with their first stone before age 20
Adults who have undergone Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, the most numerically common group affected
Patients with Crohn disease involving the terminal ileum or after ileal resection
People with coeliac disease who are not adhering to a gluten-free diet
Adults consuming very high-oxalate diets with concurrently low calcium intake
All ethnicities; primary forms show higher consanguinity prevalence in populations with traditional endogamy
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with 24-hour urine oxalate measurement; an excretion above 40 mg per day establishes the biochemical diagnosis, while excretion above 100 mg per day strongly suggests a primary genetic form. Concurrently, a full lithogenic risk panel — 24-hour calcium, citrate, uric acid, sodium, creatinine, and urine volume — contextualises the stone risk. Serum oxalate can be measured in specialist laboratories and is helpful when renal impairment limits urinary interpretation. Urine and plasma oxalate above 150-200 µmol/L are associated with systemic oxalosis. Plain abdominal radiography or low-dose CT identifies stones; renal ultrasound detects nephrocalcinosis. Distinguishing primary from enteric hyperoxaluria requires a careful history of bowel surgery or IBD, stool fat measurement or hydrogen breath testing for fat malabsorption, and, when a primary form is suspected, FMO3 gene sequencing — or more precisely AGXT, GRHPR, and HOGA1 sequencing. Liver biopsy for AGT enzyme activity measurement is the historical gold standard for PH1 but is increasingly replaced by genetic testing. Pyridoxine responsiveness testing (high-dose pyridoxine 5-10 mg/kg per day for 3-6 months, monitoring urinary oxalate) identifies the 10-30% of PH1 patients who carry the p.G170R variant and respond to pharmacological B6 doses.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The definitive quantification of urinary oxalate excretion to establish hyperoxaluria and grade severity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You collect all urine produced over exactly 24 hours into a large preservation container. Normal excretion is under 40 mg/day in adults. Values above 100 mg/day prompt genetic evaluation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative variant for genetic classification of primary hyperoxaluria types 1, 2, and 3, and guides treatment choice.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single venous blood sample is sent to a genetics laboratory. Results take several weeks. A positive result confirms the diagnosis and enables cascade testing of parents and siblings.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects kidney stones, nephrocalcinosis, and obstructive uropathy; CT is more sensitive for stone detection, ultrasound is preferred for monitoring children to limit radiation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Ultrasound is painless; CT takes a few minutes and uses X-rays but no preparation. Either can be performed urgently if obstruction is suspected.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses systemic oxalate burden when kidney function is impaired and urinary measurements underestimate accumulation; also used to monitor dialysis adequacy in end-stage disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard venous blood sample; the assay is performed in specialist metabolic laboratories and results may take several days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies PH1 patients with the p.G170R AGXT variant who will respond to pharmacological vitamin B6, potentially avoiding liver transplantation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You take high-dose pyridoxine (dose adjusted for weight) daily for 3-6 months while urinary oxalate is measured at baseline and at 3 months. A fall in excretion by more than 30% constitutes a positive response.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes enteric from primary hyperoxaluria by demonstrating fat malabsorption as the underlying mechanism.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 72-hour faecal fat collection or serum carotene test; a simpler alternative is a hydrogen breath test or measuring stool elastase to assess pancreatic function.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures calcium, citrate, uric acid, sodium, creatinine, pH, and volume alongside oxalate to quantify total stone risk and identify additional treatable risk factors.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same 24-hour urine collection as for oxalate; a single collection can be sent for the full panel. Results guide dietary and pharmaceutical recommendations.

treatmentOverview: Management of hyperoxaluria is stratified by type and severity. Across all types, the first priority is maintaining urine volume above 2-2.5 litres per day through vigorous hydration; this dilutes urinary oxalate and reduces supersaturation. Dietary oxalate restriction and ensuring adequate calcium intake (1,000-1,200 mg per day from food or supplements taken with meals) are standard for enteric and dietary hyperoxaluria. Citrate supplementation with potassium citrate or sodium citrate raises urinary citrate, which is a natural inhibitor of calcium oxalate crystal growth, and alkalinises urine to a target pH of 6.5-7.0. For primary hyperoxaluria type 1, approximately 30% of patients — particularly those with the p.G170R variant — respond to high-dose pyridoxine (vitamin B6) at 5-10 mg/kg per day, which reduces urinary oxalate by more than 30% and may prevent stone recurrence. The RNA-interference drug lumasiran (Oxlumo), approved by FDA and EMA in 2020, specifically targets hepatic glycolate oxidase and dramatically reduces urinary oxalate in PH1, with phase 3 trials showing urinary oxalate normalisation in more than 50% of patients by 6 months. For PH2, nedosiran (Rivfloza), a related RNAi drug targeting lactate dehydrogenase, received FDA approval in 2023 for patients aged 9 and older. Liver transplantation — providing a functioning AGT enzyme — corrects the underlying metabolic defect in PH1, though it has been largely supplanted by lumasiran as first-line for eligible patients. Patients who reach end-stage kidney disease require combined liver-kidney transplantation rather than kidney-only transplant, to prevent rapid recurrence in the new kidney. Urological procedures (extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy, ureteroscopy, percutaneous nephrolithotomy) address individual stones causing obstruction or renal colic.

medicalTreatments.name: Vigorous hydration
medicalTreatments.description: The single most universally recommended intervention: maintaining urine output above 2-2.5 litres per day in adults (and proportionally in children) dilutes all lithogenic factors and is the foundation of management for every hyperoxaluria type.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieving urine volume above 2 L/day reduces calcium oxalate stone recurrence by approximately 50% in observational stone-disease cohorts.

medicalTreatments.name: Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) supplementation
medicalTreatments.description: High-dose pyridoxine at 5-10 mg/kg/day corrects deficient AGT cofactor availability and reduces glyoxylate accumulation in responsive PH1 patients (those with the p.G170R variant). Response is defined as a greater than 30% reduction in urinary oxalate.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 10-30% of PH1 patients achieve major biochemical response; in responders urinary oxalate can fall by 50% or more, markedly reducing stone events.

medicalTreatments.name: Lumasiran (Oxlumo) for PH1
medicalTreatments.description: An RNA-interference therapeutic that targets hydroxyacid oxidase 1 (HAO1), reducing hepatic glycolate oxidase and thereby glyoxylate substrate delivery to the oxalate-generating pathway. Administered subcutaneously monthly (loading) then quarterly (maintenance).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In the ILLUMINATE-A phase 3 trial, 52% of adults achieved urinary oxalate normalisation at 6 months; mean urinary oxalate excretion fell by 65% from baseline.

medicalTreatments.name: Nedosiran (Rivfloza) for PH2
medicalTreatments.description: An RNAi agent targeting lactate dehydrogenase A (LDHA), which generates oxalate in the GRHPR-deficient pathway. Monthly subcutaneous injection approved for PH2 patients aged 9 and older.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In the PHYOX4 trial, nedosiran reduced urinary oxalate by approximately 54% from baseline; 36% of patients achieved urinary oxalate below the upper limit of normal.

medicalTreatments.name: Potassium citrate supplementation
medicalTreatments.description: Oral potassium citrate alkalinises urine (target pH 6.5-7.0) and raises urinary citrate, a powerful inhibitor of calcium oxalate crystallisation. Dosed to maintain 24-hour citrate excretion above 320 mg/day.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Citrate supplementation reduces stone recurrence by approximately 40-50% in calcium oxalate stone formers and is standard adjunct therapy across all hyperoxaluria types.

medicalTreatments.name: Calcium supplementation with meals
medicalTreatments.description: For enteric hyperoxaluria, calcium carbonate or calcium citrate taken with meals binds dietary oxalate in the gut lumen, reducing colonic oxalate absorption. Dose typically 1,000-2,000 mg elemental calcium daily taken at mealtimes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Dietary calcium supplementation can reduce urinary oxalate by 20-40% in enteric hyperoxaluria; it is the principal pharmacological intervention for this type.

surgicalOptions.name: Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL)
surgicalOptions.description: Non-invasive fragmentation of kidney stones smaller than 2 cm using focused shock waves delivered from outside the body, allowing the fragments to pass in the urine.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Stone-free rates of approximately 50-80% for stones under 2 cm; less effective for dense calcium oxalate monohydrate stones typical of primary hyperoxaluria.

surgicalOptions.name: Ureteroscopy and laser lithotripsy
surgicalOptions.description: A flexible ureteroscope is passed through the urethra and ureter to the stone; a holmium laser fragments the stone. Preferred for ureteral stones and for stones that are unsuitable for ESWL.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Stone-free rates above 90% for ureteral stones and 70-90% for renal pelvis stones in specialist centres.

surgicalOptions.name: Percutaneous nephrolithotomy (PCNL)
surgicalOptions.description: A working channel is created through the flank into the kidney under image guidance; stones are fragmented and evacuated. Indicated for stones larger than 2 cm or staghorn calculi.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Stone-free rates of 75-90% for stones above 2 cm in a single session; multiple sessions may be needed in severe nephrocalcinosis.

surgicalOptions.name: Combined liver-kidney transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: For primary hyperoxaluria type 1 with end-stage kidney disease, transplanting both a new liver (to correct the metabolic defect) and a kidney (to restore renal function) corrects oxalate overproduction and replaces the damaged organ. Kidney transplant alone invariably fails in untreated PH1.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Ten-year graft survival approximately 70-80% at experienced centres; outcomes have improved significantly with modern immunosuppression and the availability of lumasiran to minimise pre-transplant oxalate burden.

alternativeTreatments.name: Oxalobacter formigenes colonisation
alternativeTreatments.description: O. formigenes is an intestinal anaerobe that degrades oxalate in the gut; lower rates of colonisation correlate with higher urinary oxalate. Oral supplementation is investigational and not currently approved.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary oxalate restriction
alternativeTreatments.description: Avoiding spinach, rhubarb, almonds, peanuts, chocolate, and strong black tea reduces dietary oxalate input; useful as an adjunct in all forms, though alone insufficient for primary types.

alternativeTreatments.name: High-dose citrus intake
alternativeTreatments.description: Lemon juice provides citric acid and is widely recommended as a natural citrate adjunct; it is not a substitute for pharmaceutical potassium citrate but may modestly complement it.

whySeeSpecialist: Hyperoxaluria should be managed by a nephrologist experienced in stone disease and, for primary types, in collaboration with a metabolic geneticist and paediatric nephrologist. Specialist input is essential for genetic diagnosis, pyridoxine responsiveness testing, initiating RNA-interference therapy, and planning transplantation when kidney function declines. For enteric hyperoxaluria, gastroenterology input to optimise the underlying bowel condition runs in parallel.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a nephrologist with a subspecialty interest in kidney stone disease and metabolic nephrology; many academic renal units have dedicated stone clinics. For primary hyperoxaluria, referral to a specialist centre that participates in the OxalEurope registry or has access to lumasiran prescribing is strongly recommended. A dietitian experienced in oxalate restriction and calcium supplementation strategies should be part of the team.
preventionStrategies: Drink enough fluid to produce at least 2-2.5 litres of urine per day — the most effective single preventive measure for stone recurrence.
Ensure adequate dietary calcium intake (1,000-1,200 mg per day for adults) and take calcium supplements with meals to bind oxalate in the gut rather than on an empty stomach.
Restrict high-oxalate foods — spinach, rhubarb, almonds, peanuts, chocolate, and strong tea — particularly in primary and enteric forms.
For patients after gastric bypass: adhere to post-surgical dietary and supplement protocols, including calcium citrate with meals.
Optimise control of underlying bowel disease to minimise fat malabsorption and colonic oxalate absorption.
For primary hyperoxaluria type 1: start pyridoxine responsiveness testing early and transition to lumasiran if indicated, before significant kidney damage accrues.
Annual monitoring of 24-hour urine oxalate and renal function allows treatment intensification before kidney function deteriorates substantially.
lifestyleModifications: Carry a water bottle at all times and aim for at least 8-10 glasses of fluid daily; monitor urine colour to stay pale yellow.
Spread calcium intake throughout the day and always take calcium supplements with meals, not between them.
Read food labels for hidden oxalate sources — chocolate, tea, and nut-based products are often overlooked.
Moderate vitamin C intake to below 1,000 mg per day, as ascorbic acid is metabolised to oxalate.
Keep a dietary and symptom diary to identify foods that precede stone episodes.
Wear a medical alert bracelet identifying primary hyperoxaluria and stating that kidney-only transplant is contraindicated in PH1.

recommended: Six to eight cups of water or dilute fluids daily to maintain urine output above 2 litres
Adequate dietary calcium — low-fat dairy, fortified plant milks — taken with meals to bind gut oxalate
Moderate fruit and vegetable intake, choosing lower-oxalate options such as cabbage, broccoli, cucumber, and apple
Lemon juice or other citrus drinks to supplement urinary citrate and reduce stone supersaturation
avoid: Spinach and Swiss chard — among the highest-oxalate foods, with a single serving delivering over 700 mg oxalate
Rhubarb, which delivers extremely high oxalate loads even in small portions
Almonds, peanuts, and other nuts, which have moderate-to-high oxalate content
Strong black tea and dark chocolate — both significant oxalate sources consumed in large amounts in many diets
Vitamin C supplements above 1,000 mg per day, which are metabolised to oxalate

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise is beneficial for general health and does not worsen hyperoxaluria. Patients should ensure they compensate for sweat losses with additional fluid intake during and after exercise to maintain urine output targets. Strenuous exercise causing substantial dehydration can concentrate urine and trigger stone events; pacing fluid intake around activity is more important than avoiding exercise.
dailyManagement: Drink a measured amount of fluid each day (at least 2-2.5 litres), tracking intake if needed.
Take prescribed potassium citrate, pyridoxine, or other supplements consistently with meals as directed.
Take calcium supplements at mealtime — never between meals — so calcium is present to bind dietary oxalate in the gut.
For PH1 on lumasiran: keep a calendar for quarterly injection appointments and self-monitor urinary oxalate as directed.
Seek same-day care for sudden, severe flank pain, fever with chills, or reduced urine output, which may indicate a stone or urosepsis.
Attend annual metabolic stone clinic for 24-hour urine collection, renal function tests, and imaging review.
prognosis: Prognosis varies greatly by subtype and time of diagnosis. Dietary hyperoxaluria has an excellent prognosis with diet modification alone. For enteric hyperoxaluria, outcomes depend on successful management of the underlying bowel condition; stone recurrence can be substantially reduced with calcium supplementation and hydration. Primary hyperoxaluria type 1 historically had a poor prognosis, with approximately 50% of patients reaching end-stage kidney disease by age 25 in historical cohorts; the advent of lumasiran has dramatically changed this trajectory. Early diagnosis — ideally before significant kidney damage — and early initiation of effective therapy offer the prospect of preserved kidney function over a normal lifespan. Systemic oxalosis, once renal clearance fails, remains difficult to reverse even after transplantation. PH2 has a slower course, with end-stage kidney disease typically occurring in the fourth to sixth decade, and PH3 rarely causes significant kidney failure.
recoveryTimeline: There is no cure in the classical sense; management is lifelong. For enteric hyperoxaluria after gastric bypass, dietary modification and calcium supplementation typically normalise urinary oxalate within 4-8 weeks. Pyridoxine response in PH1 takes 3-6 months to fully assess. Lumasiran produces measurable urinary oxalate reduction within weeks, with plateau effect by 6 months. After a stone event, ureteral stenting or ureteroscopy provides relief within 24-48 hours, and most patients recover from an uncomplicated stone procedure within one to two weeks.
complications: Recurrent calcium oxalate nephrolithiasis — the most frequent complication, causing renal colic, haematuria, and risk of obstructive uropathy with each episode.
Nephrocalcinosis — diffuse calcium oxalate deposition throughout the kidney parenchyma, causing progressive chronic kidney disease even between stone events.
End-stage kidney disease — develops in the majority of patients with untreated primary hyperoxaluria type 1; may occur in the first decade of life in severe cases.
Systemic oxalosis — extra-renal crystal deposition in bone, heart, retina, vessels, and joints when plasma oxalate rises above approximately 50 µmol/L in advanced disease.
Cardiac arrhythmia and conduction block from myocardial oxalate deposition — a potentially life-threatening complication of end-stage systemic oxalosis.
Graft failure after kidney-only transplantation in PH1 — oxalate overproduction rapidly saturates and destroys the new kidney without correction of the hepatic enzyme defect.

supportResources.name: Oxalosis and Hyperoxaluria Foundation (OHF)
supportResources.description: International patient advocacy organisation providing educational resources, clinical trial listings, and peer support for patients and families affected by hyperoxaluria.

supportResources.name: National Kidney Foundation — Hyperoxaluria
supportResources.description: Patient-facing overview of primary and enteric hyperoxaluria, kidney stone risk, and treatment options from the National Kidney Foundation.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Primary Hyperoxaluria
supportResources.description: Authoritative NIH overview of primary hyperoxaluria genetics, diagnosis, and management for patients and caregivers.

sources.title: Hoppe B. An update on primary hyperoxaluria. Nature Reviews Nephrology, 2012.
Lieske JC et al. Primary hyperoxaluria type 1. GeneReviews, NCBI Bookshelf.
Garrelfs SF et al. Lumasiran, an RNAi therapeutic, for primary hyperoxaluria type 1 (ILLUMINATE-A). New England Journal of Medicine, 2021.
Nazzal L et al. Enteric hyperoxaluria after gastric bypass: mechanism and management. American Journal of Kidney Diseases, 2018.
European Association of Urology — Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2023.
OHF — Oxalosis and Hyperoxaluria Foundation clinical resources

confusedWithConditions.name: Idiopathic calcium oxalate nephrolithiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Most calcium oxalate stones occur without hyperoxaluria; isolated stone formers have normal 24-hour urine oxalate. Hyperoxaluria-associated stones typically begin earlier, are more numerous, and occur bilaterally.

confusedWithConditions.name: Primary hyperparathyroidism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hyperparathyroidism raises urinary calcium rather than oxalate, and causes hypercalcaemia; hyperoxaluria has normal or low serum calcium. Both produce calcium-containing stones, but the biochemical fingerprint differs.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gout
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gout produces uric acid stones and a distinct arthritis not seen in hyperoxaluria; urine chemistry differentiates them — elevated uric acid rather than oxalate.

relatedConditions.name: Kidney stones
relatedConditions.relevance: Calcium oxalate nephrolithiasis is the principal clinical complication of hyperoxaluria; the two conditions are directly causally linked.

relatedConditions.name: Nephrolithiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Recurrent stone formation in the collecting system is the hallmark manifestation of hyperoxaluria and drives most referrals for metabolic stone evaluation.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic kidney disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Nephrocalcinosis and recurrent obstruction from stones in primary hyperoxaluria progressively damage the kidney and culminate in chronic and end-stage kidney disease.

relatedConditions.name: Coeliac disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Untreated coeliac disease causes fat malabsorption and secondary enteric hyperoxaluria; a gluten-free diet normalises oxalate excretion.

faqs.question: What is hyperoxaluria?
faqs.answer: Hyperoxaluria is a condition where the urine contains too much oxalate — above 40 mg per day — causing calcium oxalate crystals to form in the kidneys. It can be genetic (primary hyperoxaluria) or caused by gut malabsorption (enteric) or diet.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of hyperoxaluria?
faqs.answer: The most common symptom is kidney stones causing severe flank pain (renal colic) and blood in the urine. Recurrent or bilateral stones beginning in childhood suggest primary hyperoxaluria. In severe cases, reduced kidney function develops without any stone symptoms.

faqs.question: How is hyperoxaluria diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is confirmed by a 24-hour urine collection showing oxalate above 40 mg/day. Primary hyperoxaluria is identified by AGXT, GRHPR, or HOGA1 gene sequencing. A history of bowel surgery or inflammatory bowel disease suggests enteric hyperoxaluria.

faqs.question: What foods should I avoid with hyperoxaluria?
faqs.answer: Avoid spinach, rhubarb, almonds, peanuts, dark chocolate, and strong black tea, which are very high in oxalate. Also avoid vitamin C supplements above 1,000 mg per day, which the body converts to oxalate. Adequate calcium with meals is as important as restriction.

faqs.question: Does calcium make hyperoxaluria worse?
faqs.answer: No — adequate dietary calcium from food and supplements taken with meals actually helps by binding oxalate in the gut, preventing absorption. Low calcium intake paradoxically raises urinary oxalate. Calcium should be taken at mealtimes, not between meals.

faqs.question: What is primary hyperoxaluria?
faqs.answer: Primary hyperoxaluria is a rare genetic condition caused by defects in liver enzymes that normally prevent oxalate overproduction. Type 1 (AGXT gene) is the most common and severe; types 2 and 3 are milder. All types cause recurrent kidney stones and risk kidney failure.

faqs.question: Is there a cure for primary hyperoxaluria?
faqs.answer: There is no complete cure, but the RNA-interference drug lumasiran (for type 1) and nedosiran (for type 2) can normalise urinary oxalate in many patients without surgery. Liver transplantation corrects the enzyme defect in type 1 but has largely been replaced by these targeted therapies.

faqs.question: What is enteric hyperoxaluria?
faqs.answer: Enteric hyperoxaluria occurs when fat malabsorption — from gastric bypass surgery, Crohn disease, or coeliac disease — leaves excess fatty acids in the colon that bind calcium, freeing oxalate for absorption. The result is raised urinary oxalate and calcium oxalate stones.

faqs.question: Can gastric bypass surgery cause kidney stones?
faqs.answer: Yes. Roux-en-Y gastric bypass causes fat malabsorption in up to 40% of patients, triggering enteric hyperoxaluria and raising calcium oxalate stone risk three to four times above the general population. Calcium citrate with meals and dietary oxalate restriction are the key preventive measures.

faqs.question: How much water should I drink with hyperoxaluria?
faqs.answer: Aim to produce at least 2 to 2.5 litres of urine per day as an adult. This usually requires drinking 2.5 to 3 litres of fluid. Pale yellow urine is a practical guide to adequate hydration. This is the single most effective preventive measure for stone recurrence.

faqs.question: What is systemic oxalosis?
faqs.answer: Systemic oxalosis occurs when the kidneys cannot clear oxalate fast enough and plasma levels rise, causing calcium oxalate crystals to deposit in bone, heart, retina, and blood vessels. It is a serious complication of advanced primary hyperoxaluria and requires urgent specialist management.

faqs.question: Why does hyperoxaluria cause kidney failure?
faqs.answer: Calcium oxalate crystals deposit throughout the kidney (nephrocalcinosis) and obstruct tubules, provoking inflammation and scarring. Repeated stone obstruction also damages nephrons. In primary hyperoxaluria type 1 the oxalate load is high enough to cause renal failure in children without treatment.

faqs.question: What is lumasiran and how does it work?
faqs.answer: Lumasiran (Oxlumo) is an RNA-interference drug approved for primary hyperoxaluria type 1. It silences the gene encoding glycolate oxidase in the liver, reducing the substrate available to make oxalate. It is injected under the skin monthly then quarterly and reduced urinary oxalate by 65% on average in clinical trials.

faqs.question: Can hyperoxaluria affect children?
faqs.answer: Yes — primary hyperoxaluria types 1 and 2 frequently present in childhood with recurrent kidney stones or nephrocalcinosis, and can cause kidney failure before adulthood in severe cases. Early genetic testing and treatment initiation before significant kidney damage have dramatically improved paediatric outcomes.

faqs.question: Does hyperoxaluria always cause kidney stones?
faqs.answer: High urinary oxalate markedly raises stone risk but not everyone develops symptoms. Some patients have nephrocalcinosis — diffuse crystal deposition within the kidney — without discrete stones, and may present with gradually declining kidney function rather than colic.

faqs.question: Is hyperoxaluria hereditary?
faqs.answer: The primary types are autosomal recessive: both parents must carry a defective gene copy for a child to be affected, giving a 1 in 4 chance per pregnancy. Enteric and dietary hyperoxaluria are not inherited. Genetic testing of siblings and children of PH1 patients is recommended.

faqs.question: Can hyperoxaluria be managed with diet alone?
faqs.answer: Dietary hyperoxaluria often responds to diet modification and hydration alone. Primary hyperoxaluria type 1 cannot be managed adequately with diet; it requires medication such as lumasiran or pyridoxine. Enteric hyperoxaluria needs calcium supplementation and treatment of the underlying bowel problem in addition to diet.

faqs.question: What is the role of vitamin B6 in hyperoxaluria?
faqs.answer: High-dose vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) is effective in the 10-30% of primary hyperoxaluria type 1 patients who carry the p.G170R AGXT variant. It acts as a cofactor that stabilises the enzyme, reducing glyoxylate to oxalate conversion. A 3-6 month trial is recommended for all newly diagnosed PH1 patients.

faqs.question: What specialist treats hyperoxaluria?
faqs.answer: A nephrologist subspecialising in kidney stone disease and metabolic nephrology leads management, with input from a metabolic geneticist for primary types and a gastroenterologist for enteric types. Paediatric nephrology is essential when children are affected. Specialist stone clinics coordinate care.

faqs.question: When does hyperoxaluria need a kidney transplant?
faqs.answer: For primary hyperoxaluria type 1 reaching end-stage kidney disease, kidney transplant alone consistently fails because the liver continues overproducing oxalate. Combined liver-kidney transplantation is required, though newer RNA-interference therapies like lumasiran aim to preserve kidney function before transplant becomes necessary.

metaDescription: Hyperoxaluria causes excess urinary oxalate and recurrent kidney stones. Learn about primary types, enteric causes, diagnosis, and lumasiran treatment options.
